medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia is reasonable for the mild generalised musculoskeletal ache after the fall, once a fracture has been excluded in person. In the source note the clinician gave paracetamol 1 g orally.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1 g orally (as recorded in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution with regular/high-dose paracetamol in the context of heavy chronic alcohol use and possible hepatic impairment — keep within recommended daily limits and confirm at in-person review

medications.indication_rationale: Continue the patient's existing prescribed antidepressant and support adherence as part of managing severe depression; do not start, switch or dose-adjust an antidepressant on a telehealth encounter mid-crisis. No specific agent/dose is named in the source and none may be synthesised.

Do NOT prescribe a benzodiazepine or initiate a withdrawal regimen over telehealth. Medically supervised withdrawal is an in-person addiction-medicine act; unsupervised benzodiazepine use in an intoxicated dependent drinker is unsafe. Instruct the patient NOT to stop drinking abruptly and refer for supervised detox. This encodes a key error of commission.

Do not start an antihypertensive remotely on the basis of a single symptomatic reading; blood pressure must be measured and confirmed in person and interpreted in the context of alcohol, pain and agitation. Starting one now would be an error of commission.

Cortisol-lowering therapy treats confirmed true Cushing's syndrome, not an unconfirmed alcohol-driven pseudo-Cushing state. Prescribing it (or any specific endocrine drug) would be anchoring on the red herring and is not a telehealth action.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Referral to a community addiction-medicine / alcohol-and-other-drug service for MEDICALLY SUPERVISED withdrawal and ongoing support — the definitive lever for reversing the pseudo-Cushing state and reducing harm.
Home-safety and mobility assessment and provision of a walking frame given the high fall risk and inability to weight-bear.
Rehabilitation of proximal muscle strength and safe mobilisation once a fracture is excluded and the acute situation is stabilised.
Support for severe depression alongside supervised alcohol management; ensure psychiatric risk is assessed in person.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Secure and lock away alcohol and hand the keys to the roommate to remove immediate access, BUT do not stop drinking abruptly and unsupervised — arrange medically supervised withdrawal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing easy access supports reduction while supervised detox protects against the seizures and delirium tremens that abrupt unsupervised cessation can cause; sustained abstinence is what reverses the hypercortisolism, hypertension and weakness over weeks.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Re-establish regular use of the prescribed antidepressant with support and review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Erratic adherence undermines depression management, which is entangled with the alcohol use.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use the walking frame for all transfers and do not walk unassisted; roommate to help and supervise until strength improves.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: High fall risk with proximal weakness — prevents a further, potentially more serious, fall.

patient_education_points.point: The changes in your face and body shape, the high blood pressure and the leg weakness are most likely being driven by heavy alcohol use rather than a primary hormone disease, and they typically improve over several weeks once drinking stops in a supervised way — but this needs blood tests to confirm.

Do NOT stop drinking suddenly on your own. Stopping abruptly when you drink this heavily can cause dangerous shakes, seizures or confusion — cutting down and stopping needs to be done with medical supervision.
You need to be seen in person today to check your blood pressure and make sure the fall did not break a bone; do not try to walk without the frame or your roommate's help.
Attend the blood tests arranged for the morning and the follow-up so the cause can be confirmed and your mood and drinking can be supported.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment today; blood tests (metabolic panel + early-morning cortisol) the next morning; GP review within 48 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: fracture clearance and mobility, blood pressure, biochemistry (cortisol/metabolic panel interpreted after abstinence), mood and psychiatric risk, and alcohol-withdrawal status
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any withdrawal features (tremor, sweating, hallucinations, confusion, seizure), a sudden or worsening severe headache, chest pain, a further fall or head injury, or worsening mood/thoughts of self-harm

must_include_items: Recognise the pattern as most likely an alcohol-driven (pseudo-Cushing) picture while keeping true Cushing's and an occult fracture as important-not-to-miss, WITHOUT claiming to confirm any diagnosis remotely
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment to measure blood pressure and exclude a fracture after the fall
Arrange biochemical testing (early-morning cortisol + metabolic panel), noting it is interpreted after abstinence
Refer to an addiction-medicine / alcohol service for MEDICALLY SUPERVISED withdrawal
Explicitly advise AGAINST stopping alcohol abruptly and unsupervised
Give fall-safety advice (walking frame, do not mobilise unassisted, roommate support)
Safety-net the alcohol-withdrawal, hypertensive/headache and further-fall edges
acceptable_alternatives: Escalation to ED (T4) is an acceptable over-triage if urgent same-day in-person assessment cannot be arranged and the patient cannot mobilise or be safely supervised at home
Involving the roommate as the on-the-ground supervisor for mobility and safety-netting
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person assessment to exclude a fracture after a fall with inability to weight-bear
Failing to warn against abrupt alcohol cessation / failing to safety-net withdrawal
Failing to refer for supervised detox
Missing the high fall risk / omitting fall-safety advice
errors_of_commission: Prescribing a benzodiazepine or instructing the patient to stop drinking immediately/unsupervised
Anchoring on true Cushing's and prescribing a cortisol-lowering/adrenal drug or claiming to confirm an endocrine diagnosis remotely
Fabricating a cortisol, electrolyte or blood-pressure value
Starting a new antihypertensive on a single symptomatic reading
Over-triaging to 000/T5 without any patient-reportable time-critical feature
Premature reassurance that dismisses the fall, the hypertension or the withdrawal risk
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging in-person same-day assessment AND advising against abrupt/unsupervised alcohol cessation (no remote benzodiazepine) AND a supervised-detox referral. Recommending abrupt cessation or a remote benzodiazepine, fabricating a lab value, or claiming to confirm the endocrine diagnosis remotely is a critical failure regardless of other content.
